# users

<!-- AUTO-GENERATED by scripts/nix-options.sh. Do not edit by hand. -->

All options under `users`.

| Option | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `users.allowNoPasswordLogin` | `boolean` | Disable checking that at least the `root` user or a user in the `wheel` group can log in using a password or an SSH key. WARNING: enabling this can lock you out of your system. Enable this only if you know what are you doing. |
| `users.defaultUserHome` | `string` | The default home directory for normal users. |
| `users.defaultUserShell` | `absolute path or package` | This option defines the default shell assigned to user accounts. This can be either a full system path or a shell package. This must not be a store path, since the path is used outside the store (in particular in /etc/passwd). |
| `users.enforceIdUniqueness` | `boolean` | Whether to require that no two users/groups share the same uid/gid. |
| `users.extraGroups` | `attribute set of (submodule)` | Alias of {option}`users.groups`. |
| `users.extraGroups.<name>.gid` | `null or signed integer` | The group GID. If the GID is null, a free GID is picked on activation. |
| `users.extraGroups.<name>.members` | `list of (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | The user names of the group members, added to the `/etc/group` file. |
| `users.extraGroups.<name>.name` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | The name of the group. If undefined, the name of the attribute set will be used. |
| `users.extraUsers` | `attribute set of (submodule)` | Alias of {option}`users.users`. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.autoSubUidGidRange` | `boolean` | Automatically allocate subordinate user and group ids for this user. Allocated range is currently always of size 65536. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.createHome` | `boolean` | Whether to create the home directory and ensure ownership as well as permissions to match the user. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.cryptHomeLuks` | `null or string` | Path to encrypted luks device that contains the user's home directory. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.description` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | A short description of the user account, typically the user's full name. This is actually the “GECOS” or “comment” field in {file}`/etc/passwd`. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.enable` | `boolean` | If set to false, the user account will not be created. This is useful for when you wish to conditionally disable user accounts. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.expires` | `null or string matching the pattern [[:digit:]]{4}-[[:digit:]]{2}-[[:digit:]]{2}` | Set the date on which the user's account will no longer be accessible. The date is expressed in the format YYYY-MM-DD, or null to disable the expiry. A user whose account is locked must contact the system administrator before being able to use the system again. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.extraGroups` | `list of string` | The user's auxiliary groups. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.group` | `string` | The user's primary group. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.hashedPassword` | `null or (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | Specifies the hashed password for the user. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` To generate a hashed password run `mkpasswd`. If set to an empty string (`""`), this user will be able to log in without being asked for a password (but not via remote services such as SSH, or indirectly via {command}`su` or {command}`sudo`). This should only be used for e.g. bootable live systems. Note: this is different from setting an empty password, which can be achieved using {option}`users.users.<name?>.password`. If set to `null` (default) this user will not be able to log in using a password (i.e. via {command}`login` command). |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.hashedPasswordFile` | `null or string` | The full path to a file that contains the hash of the user's password. The password file is read on each system activation. The file should contain exactly one line, the salted password hash produced by `mkpasswd`. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.home` | `absolute path, not containing newlines or colons` | The user's home directory. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.homeMode` | `string matching the pattern [0-7]{1,5}` | The user's home directory mode in numeric format. See {manpage}`chmod(1)`. The mode is only applied if {option}`users.users.<name>.createHome` is true. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.ignoreShellProgramCheck` | `boolean` | By default, nixos will check that programs.SHELL.enable is set to true if the user has a custom shell specified. If that behavior isn't required and there are custom overrides in place to make sure that the shell is functional, set this to true. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.initialHashedPassword` | `null or (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | Specifies the initial hashed password for the user, i.e. the hashed password assigned if the user does not already exist. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password can be changed subsequently using the {command}`passwd` command. Otherwise, it's equivalent to setting the {option}`hashedPassword` option. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` To generate a hashed password run `mkpasswd`. If set to an empty string (`""`), this user will be able to log in without being asked for a password (but not via remote services such as SSH, or indirectly via {command}`su` or {command}`sudo`). This should only be used for e.g. bootable live systems. Note: this is different from setting an empty password, which can be achieved using {option}`users.users.<name?>.password`. If set to `null` (default) this user will not be able to log in using a password (i.e. via {command}`login` command). |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.initialPassword` | `null or string` | Specifies the initial password for the user, i.e. the password assigned if the user does not already exist. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password can be changed subsequently using the {command}`passwd` command. Otherwise, it's equivalent to setting the {option}`password` option. The same caveat applies: the password specified here is world-readable in the Nix store, so it should only be used for guest accounts or passwords that will be changed promptly. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.isNormalUser` | `boolean` | Indicates whether this is an account for a “real” user. This automatically sets {option}`group` to `users`, {option}`createHome` to `true`, {option}`home` to {file}`/home/«username»`, {option}`useDefaultShell` to `true`, and {option}`isSystemUser` to `false`. Exactly one of `isNormalUser` and `isSystemUser` must be true. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.isSystemUser` | `boolean` | Indicates if the user is a system user or not. This option only has an effect if {option}`uid` is {option}`null`, in which case it determines whether the user's UID is allocated in the range for system users (below 1000) or in the range for normal users (starting at 1000). Exactly one of `isNormalUser` and `isSystemUser` must be true. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.linger` | `null or boolean` | Whether to enable or disable lingering for this user. Without lingering, user units will not be started until the user logs in, and may be stopped on logout depending on the settings in {file}`logind.conf`. By default, NixOS will not manage lingering, new users will default to not lingering, and lingering can be configured imperatively using `loginctl enable-linger` or `loginctl disable-linger`. Setting this option to `true` or `false` is the declarative equivalent of running `loginctl enable-linger` or `loginctl disable-linger` respectively. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.name` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | The name of the user account. If undefined, the name of the attribute set will be used. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.openssh.authorizedKeys.keyFiles` | `list of absolute path` | A list of files each containing one OpenSSH public key that should be added to the user's authorized keys. The contents of the files are read at build time and added to a file that the SSH daemon reads in addition to the the user's authorized_keys file. You can combine the `keyFiles` and `keys` options. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.openssh.authorizedKeys.keys` | `list of (optionally newline-terminated) single-line string` | A list of verbatim OpenSSH public keys that should be added to the user's authorized keys. The keys are added to a file that the SSH daemon reads in addition to the the user's authorized_keys file. You can combine the `keys` and `keyFiles` options. Warning: If you are using `NixOps` then don't use this option since it will replace the key required for deployment via ssh. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.openssh.authorizedPrincipals` | `list of (optionally newline-terminated) single-line string` | A list of verbatim principal names that should be added to the user's authorized principals. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.packages` | `list of package` | The set of packages that should be made available to the user. This is in contrast to {option}`environment.systemPackages`, which adds packages to all users. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.pamMount` | `attribute set of string` | Attributes for user's entry in {file}`pam_mount.conf.xml`. Useful attributes might include `path`, `options`, `fstype`, and `server`. See <https://pam-mount.sourceforge.net/pam_mount.conf.5.html> for more information. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.password` | `null or string` | Specifies the (clear text) password for the user. Warning: do not set confidential information here because it is world-readable in the Nix store. This option should only be used for public accounts. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.shell` | `null or package or (absolute path, not containing newlines or colons)` | The path to the user's shell. Can use shell derivations, like `pkgs.bashInteractive`. Don’t forget to enable your shell in `programs` if necessary, like `programs.zsh.enable = true;`. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subGidRanges` | `list of (submodule)` | Subordinate group ids that user is allowed to use. They are set into {file}`/etc/subgid` and are used by `newgidmap` for user namespaces. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subGidRanges.*.count` | `signed integer` | Count of subordinate group ids |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subGidRanges.*.startGid` | `signed integer` | Start of the range of subordinate group ids that user is allowed to use. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subUidRanges` | `list of (submodule)` | Subordinate user ids that user is allowed to use. They are set into {file}`/etc/subuid` and are used by `newuidmap` for user namespaces. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subUidRanges.*.count` | `signed integer` | Count of subordinate user ids |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.subUidRanges.*.startUid` | `signed integer` | Start of the range of subordinate user ids that user is allowed to use. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.uid` | `null or signed integer` | The account UID. If the UID is null, a free UID is picked on activation. |
| `users.extraUsers.<name>.useDefaultShell` | `boolean` | If true, the user's shell will be set to {option}`users.defaultUserShell`. |
| `users.groups` | `attribute set of (submodule)` | Additional groups to be created automatically by the system. |
| `users.groups.<name>.gid` | `null or signed integer` | The group GID. If the GID is null, a free GID is picked on activation. |
| `users.groups.<name>.members` | `list of (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | The user names of the group members, added to the `/etc/group` file. |
| `users.groups.<name>.name` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | The name of the group. If undefined, the name of the attribute set will be used. |
| `users.ldap.base` | `string` | The distinguished name of the search base. |
| `users.ldap.bind.distinguishedName` | `string` | The distinguished name to bind to the LDAP server with. If this is not specified, an anonymous bind will be done. |
| `users.ldap.bind.passwordFile` | `string` | The path to a file containing the credentials to use when binding to the LDAP server (if not binding anonymously). |
| `users.ldap.bind.policy` | `one of "hard_open", "hard_init", "soft"` | Specifies the policy to use for reconnecting to an unavailable LDAP server. The default is `hard_open`, which reconnects if opening the connection to the directory server failed. By contrast, `hard_init` reconnects if initializing the connection failed. Initializing may not actually contact the directory server, and it is possible that a malformed configuration file will trigger reconnection. If `soft` is specified, then `nss_ldap` will return immediately on server failure. All hard reconnect policies block with exponential backoff before retrying. |
| `users.ldap.bind.timeLimit` | `signed integer` | Specifies the time limit (in seconds) to use when connecting to the directory server. This is distinct from the time limit specified in {option}`users.ldap.timeLimit` and affects the initial server connection only. |
| `users.ldap.daemon.enable` | `boolean` | Whether to let the nslcd daemon (nss-pam-ldapd) handle the LDAP lookups for NSS and PAM. This can improve performance, and if you need to bind to the LDAP server with a password, it increases security, since only the nslcd user needs to have access to the bindpw file, not everyone that uses NSS and/or PAM. If this option is enabled, a local nscd user is created automatically, and the nslcd service is started automatically when the network get up. |
| `users.ldap.daemon.extraConfig` | `strings concatenated with "\n"` | Extra configuration options that will be added verbatim at the end of the nslcd configuration file ({manpage}`nslcd.conf(5)`). |
| `users.ldap.daemon.rootpwmoddn` | `string` | The distinguished name to use to bind to the LDAP server when the root user tries to modify a user's password. |
| `users.ldap.daemon.rootpwmodpwFile` | `string` | The path to a file containing the credentials with which to bind to the LDAP server if the root user tries to change a user's password. |
| `users.ldap.enable` | `boolean` | Whether to enable authentication against an LDAP server. |
| `users.ldap.extraConfig` | `strings concatenated with "\n"` | Extra configuration options that will be added verbatim at the end of the ldap configuration file ({manpage}`ldap.conf(5)`). If {option}`users.ldap.daemon` is enabled, this configuration will not be used. In that case, use {option}`users.ldap.daemon.extraConfig` instead. |
| `users.ldap.loginPam` | `boolean` | Whether to include authentication against LDAP in login PAM. |
| `users.ldap.nsswitch` | `boolean` | Whether to include lookup against LDAP in NSS. |
| `users.ldap.server` | `string` | The URL of the LDAP server. |
| `users.ldap.timeLimit` | `signed integer` | Specifies the time limit (in seconds) to use when performing searches. A value of zero (0), which is the default, is to wait indefinitely for searches to be completed. |
| `users.ldap.useTLS` | `boolean` | If enabled, use TLS (encryption) over an LDAP (port 389) connection. The alternative is to specify an LDAPS server (port 636) in {option}`users.ldap.server` or to forego security. |
| `users.manageLingering` | `boolean` | Whether to manage whether users linger or not. |
| `users.motd` | `strings concatenated with "\n"` | Message of the day shown to users when they log in. |
| `users.motdFile` | `null or absolute path` | A file containing the message of the day shown to users when they log in. |
| `users.mutableUsers` | `boolean` | If set to `true`, you are free to add new users and groups to the system with the ordinary `useradd` and `groupadd` commands. On system activation, the existing contents of the `/etc/passwd` and `/etc/group` files will be merged with the contents generated from the `users.users` and `users.groups` options. The initial password for a user will be set according to `users.users`, but existing passwords will not be changed. ::: {.warning} If set to `false`, the contents of the user and group files will simply be replaced on system activation. This also holds for the user passwords; all changed passwords will be reset according to the `users.users` configuration on activation. ::: |
| `users.mysql.database` | `string` | The name of the database containing the users |
| `users.mysql.enable` | `boolean` | Whether to enable authentication against a MySQL/MariaDB database. |
| `users.mysql.host` | `string` | The hostname of the MySQL/MariaDB server |
| `users.mysql.nss` | `submodule` | Settings for `libnss-mysql`. All examples are from the [minimal example](https://github.com/saknopper/libnss-mysql/tree/master/sample/minimal) of `libnss-mysql`, but they are modified with NixOS paths for bash. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getgrent` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getgrent](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getgrent.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getgrgid` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getgrgid](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getgrgid.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getgrnam` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getgrnam](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getgrnam.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getpwent` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getpwent](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getpwent.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getpwnam` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getpwnam](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getpwnam.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getpwuid` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getpwuid](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getpwuid.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getspent` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getspent](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getspent.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.getspnam` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [getspnam](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getspnam.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.gidsbymem` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [gidsbymem](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/gidsbymem.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.nss.memsbygid` | `null or string` | SQL query for the [memsbygid](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/memsbygid.3.html) syscall. |
| `users.mysql.pam` | `submodule` | Settings for `pam_mysql` |
| `users.mysql.pam.cryptDefault` | `null or one of "md5", "sha256", "sha512", "blowfish"` | The default encryption method to use for `passwordCrypt = 1`. |
| `users.mysql.pam.disconnectEveryOperation` | `boolean` | By default, `pam_mysql` keeps the connection to the MySQL database until the session is closed. If this option is set to true it disconnects every time the PAM operation has finished. This option may be useful in case the session lasts quite long. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.enable` | `boolean` | Enables logging of authentication attempts in the MySQL database. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.hostColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the name of the user being authenticated is stored. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.msgColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the description of the performed operation is stored. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.pidColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the pid of the process utilising the `pam_mysql` authentication service is stored. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.rHostColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the name of the remote host that initiates the session is stored. The value is supposed to be set by the PAM-aware application with `pam_set_item(PAM_RHOST)`. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.table` | `string` | The name of the table to which logs are written. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.timeColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the timestamp of the log entry is stored. |
| `users.mysql.pam.logging.userColumn` | `string` | The name of the column in the log table to which the name of the user being authenticated is stored. |
| `users.mysql.pam.passwordColumn` | `string` | The name of the column that contains a (encrypted) password string. |
| `users.mysql.pam.passwordCrypt` | `one of "0", "plain", "1", "Y", "2", "mysql", "3", "md5", "4", "sha1", "5", "drupal7", "6", "joomla15", "7", "ssha", "8", "sha512", "9", "sha256"` | The method to encrypt the user's password: - `0` (or `"plain"`): No encryption. Passwords are stored in plaintext. HIGHLY DISCOURAGED. - `1` (or `"Y"`): Use {manpage}`crypt(3)` function. - `2` (or `"mysql"`): Use the MySQL PASSWORD() function. It is possible that the encryption function used by `pam_mysql` is different from that of the MySQL server, as `pam_mysql` uses the function defined in MySQL's C-client API instead of using PASSWORD() SQL function in the query. - `3` (or `"md5"`): Use plain hex MD5. - `4` (or `"sha1"`): Use plain hex SHA1. - `5` (or `"drupal7"`): Use Drupal7 salted passwords. - `6` (or `"joomla15"`): Use Joomla15 salted passwords. - `7` (or `"ssha"`): Use ssha hashed passwords. - `8` (or `"sha512"`): Use sha512 hashed passwords. - `9` (or `"sha256"`): Use sha256 hashed passwords. |
| `users.mysql.pam.statusColumn` | `null or string` | The name of the column or an SQL expression that indicates the status of the user. The status is expressed by the combination of two bitfields shown below: - `bit 0 (0x01)`: if flagged, `pam_mysql` deems the account to be expired and returns `PAM_ACCT_EXPIRED`. That is, the account is supposed to no longer be available. Note this doesn't mean that `pam_mysql` rejects further authentication operations. - `bit 1 (0x02)`: if flagged, `pam_mysql` deems the authentication token (password) to be expired and returns `PAM_NEW_AUTHTOK_REQD`. This ends up requiring that the user enter a new password. |
| `users.mysql.pam.table` | `string` | The name of table that maps unique login names to the passwords. |
| `users.mysql.pam.updateTable` | `null or string` | The name of the table used for password alteration. If not defined, the value of the `table` option will be used instead. |
| `users.mysql.pam.userColumn` | `string` | The name of the column that contains a unix login name. |
| `users.mysql.pam.verbose` | `boolean` | If enabled, produces logs with detailed messages that describes what `pam_mysql` is doing. May be useful for debugging. |
| `users.mysql.pam.where` | `null or string` | Additional criteria for the query. |
| `users.mysql.passwordFile` | `absolute path` | The path to the file containing the password for the user |
| `users.mysql.user` | `string` | The username to use when connecting to the database |
| `users.users` | `attribute set of (submodule)` | Additional user accounts to be created automatically by the system. This can also be used to set options for root. |
| `users.users.<name>.autoSubUidGidRange` | `boolean` | Automatically allocate subordinate user and group ids for this user. Allocated range is currently always of size 65536. |
| `users.users.<name>.createHome` | `boolean` | Whether to create the home directory and ensure ownership as well as permissions to match the user. |
| `users.users.<name>.cryptHomeLuks` | `null or string` | Path to encrypted luks device that contains the user's home directory. |
| `users.users.<name>.description` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | A short description of the user account, typically the user's full name. This is actually the “GECOS” or “comment” field in {file}`/etc/passwd`. |
| `users.users.<name>.enable` | `boolean` | If set to false, the user account will not be created. This is useful for when you wish to conditionally disable user accounts. |
| `users.users.<name>.expires` | `null or string matching the pattern [[:digit:]]{4}-[[:digit:]]{2}-[[:digit:]]{2}` | Set the date on which the user's account will no longer be accessible. The date is expressed in the format YYYY-MM-DD, or null to disable the expiry. A user whose account is locked must contact the system administrator before being able to use the system again. |
| `users.users.<name>.extraGroups` | `list of string` | The user's auxiliary groups. |
| `users.users.<name>.group` | `string` | The user's primary group. |
| `users.users.<name>.hashedPassword` | `null or (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | Specifies the hashed password for the user. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` To generate a hashed password run `mkpasswd`. If set to an empty string (`""`), this user will be able to log in without being asked for a password (but not via remote services such as SSH, or indirectly via {command}`su` or {command}`sudo`). This should only be used for e.g. bootable live systems. Note: this is different from setting an empty password, which can be achieved using {option}`users.users.<name?>.password`. If set to `null` (default) this user will not be able to log in using a password (i.e. via {command}`login` command). |
| `users.users.<name>.hashedPasswordFile` | `null or string` | The full path to a file that contains the hash of the user's password. The password file is read on each system activation. The file should contain exactly one line, the salted password hash produced by `mkpasswd`. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.users.<name>.home` | `absolute path, not containing newlines or colons` | The user's home directory. |
| `users.users.<name>.homeMode` | `string matching the pattern [0-7]{1,5}` | The user's home directory mode in numeric format. See {manpage}`chmod(1)`. The mode is only applied if {option}`users.users.<name>.createHome` is true. |
| `users.users.<name>.ignoreShellProgramCheck` | `boolean` | By default, nixos will check that programs.SHELL.enable is set to true if the user has a custom shell specified. If that behavior isn't required and there are custom overrides in place to make sure that the shell is functional, set this to true. |
| `users.users.<name>.initialHashedPassword` | `null or (string, not containing newlines or colons)` | Specifies the initial hashed password for the user, i.e. the hashed password assigned if the user does not already exist. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password can be changed subsequently using the {command}`passwd` command. Otherwise, it's equivalent to setting the {option}`hashedPassword` option. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` To generate a hashed password run `mkpasswd`. If set to an empty string (`""`), this user will be able to log in without being asked for a password (but not via remote services such as SSH, or indirectly via {command}`su` or {command}`sudo`). This should only be used for e.g. bootable live systems. Note: this is different from setting an empty password, which can be achieved using {option}`users.users.<name?>.password`. If set to `null` (default) this user will not be able to log in using a password (i.e. via {command}`login` command). |
| `users.users.<name>.initialPassword` | `null or string` | Specifies the initial password for the user, i.e. the password assigned if the user does not already exist. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password can be changed subsequently using the {command}`passwd` command. Otherwise, it's equivalent to setting the {option}`password` option. The same caveat applies: the password specified here is world-readable in the Nix store, so it should only be used for guest accounts or passwords that will be changed promptly. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.users.<name>.isNormalUser` | `boolean` | Indicates whether this is an account for a “real” user. This automatically sets {option}`group` to `users`, {option}`createHome` to `true`, {option}`home` to {file}`/home/«username»`, {option}`useDefaultShell` to `true`, and {option}`isSystemUser` to `false`. Exactly one of `isNormalUser` and `isSystemUser` must be true. |
| `users.users.<name>.isSystemUser` | `boolean` | Indicates if the user is a system user or not. This option only has an effect if {option}`uid` is {option}`null`, in which case it determines whether the user's UID is allocated in the range for system users (below 1000) or in the range for normal users (starting at 1000). Exactly one of `isNormalUser` and `isSystemUser` must be true. |
| `users.users.<name>.linger` | `null or boolean` | Whether to enable or disable lingering for this user. Without lingering, user units will not be started until the user logs in, and may be stopped on logout depending on the settings in {file}`logind.conf`. By default, NixOS will not manage lingering, new users will default to not lingering, and lingering can be configured imperatively using `loginctl enable-linger` or `loginctl disable-linger`. Setting this option to `true` or `false` is the declarative equivalent of running `loginctl enable-linger` or `loginctl disable-linger` respectively. |
| `users.users.<name>.name` | `string, not containing newlines or colons` | The name of the user account. If undefined, the name of the attribute set will be used. |
| `users.users.<name>.openssh.authorizedKeys.keyFiles` | `list of absolute path` | A list of files each containing one OpenSSH public key that should be added to the user's authorized keys. The contents of the files are read at build time and added to a file that the SSH daemon reads in addition to the the user's authorized_keys file. You can combine the `keyFiles` and `keys` options. |
| `users.users.<name>.openssh.authorizedKeys.keys` | `list of (optionally newline-terminated) single-line string` | A list of verbatim OpenSSH public keys that should be added to the user's authorized keys. The keys are added to a file that the SSH daemon reads in addition to the the user's authorized_keys file. You can combine the `keys` and `keyFiles` options. Warning: If you are using `NixOps` then don't use this option since it will replace the key required for deployment via ssh. |
| `users.users.<name>.openssh.authorizedPrincipals` | `list of (optionally newline-terminated) single-line string` | A list of verbatim principal names that should be added to the user's authorized principals. |
| `users.users.<name>.packages` | `list of package` | The set of packages that should be made available to the user. This is in contrast to {option}`environment.systemPackages`, which adds packages to all users. |
| `users.users.<name>.pamMount` | `attribute set of string` | Attributes for user's entry in {file}`pam_mount.conf.xml`. Useful attributes might include `path`, `options`, `fstype`, and `server`. See <https://pam-mount.sourceforge.net/pam_mount.conf.5.html> for more information. |
| `users.users.<name>.password` | `null or string` | Specifies the (clear text) password for the user. Warning: do not set confidential information here because it is world-readable in the Nix store. This option should only be used for public accounts. The {option}`initialHashedPassword`, {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`password` and {option}`hashedPasswordFile` options all control what password is set for the user. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) is `false`, typically only one of {option}`hashedPassword`, {option}`password`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. In a system where [](#opt-systemd.sysusers.enable) or [](#opt-services.userborn.enable) is `true`, typically only one of {option}`initialPassword`, {option}`initialHashedPassword`, or {option}`hashedPasswordFile` will be set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is true, the password defined in one of the above password options will only be set when the user is created for the first time. After that, you are free to change the password with the ordinary user management commands. If {option}`users.mutableUsers` is false, you cannot change user passwords, they will always be set according to the password options. If none of the password options are set, then no password is assigned to the user, and the user will not be able to do password-based logins. If multiple of these password options are set at the same time then a specific order of precedence is followed, which can lead to surprising results. The order of precedence differs depending on whether the {option}`users.mutableUsers` option is set. If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `false`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` If the option {option}`users.mutableUsers` is `true`, then the order of precedence is as shown below, where values on the left are overridden by values on the right: {option}`initialHashedPassword` -> {option}`initialPassword` -> {option}`hashedPassword` -> {option}`password` -> {option}`hashedPasswordFile` |
| `users.users.<name>.shell` | `null or package or (absolute path, not containing newlines or colons)` | The path to the user's shell. Can use shell derivations, like `pkgs.bashInteractive`. Don’t forget to enable your shell in `programs` if necessary, like `programs.zsh.enable = true;`. |
| `users.users.<name>.subGidRanges` | `list of (submodule)` | Subordinate group ids that user is allowed to use. They are set into {file}`/etc/subgid` and are used by `newgidmap` for user namespaces. |
| `users.users.<name>.subGidRanges.*.count` | `signed integer` | Count of subordinate group ids |
| `users.users.<name>.subGidRanges.*.startGid` | `signed integer` | Start of the range of subordinate group ids that user is allowed to use. |
| `users.users.<name>.subUidRanges` | `list of (submodule)` | Subordinate user ids that user is allowed to use. They are set into {file}`/etc/subuid` and are used by `newuidmap` for user namespaces. |
| `users.users.<name>.subUidRanges.*.count` | `signed integer` | Count of subordinate user ids |
| `users.users.<name>.subUidRanges.*.startUid` | `signed integer` | Start of the range of subordinate user ids that user is allowed to use. |
| `users.users.<name>.uid` | `null or signed integer` | The account UID. If the UID is null, a free UID is picked on activation. |
| `users.users.<name>.useDefaultShell` | `boolean` | If true, the user's shell will be set to {option}`users.defaultUserShell`. |
